---
title: "Syllabus"
format: html
---

::: callout-note

Building on previous sessions:

- Regression I builds the basics, Regression II introduces model tuning (forward/backward variable selection in multiple regression, non-linear transformations, logistic regression)
- ML I builds the basics, ML II introduces feature engineering, ensemble models, ...

:::

## Summary

This module introduces students to the methods, technologies, and governance of modern data analytics across descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive approaches.
It builds a comprehensive understanding of the data analytics process—from data structuring and preparation to exploratory data analysis, analytical modeling, and decision support.
The module combines conceptual foundations and hands-on analytical practice using Python, pandas, and scikit-learn.
It progresses from structured transactional data (data warehouses, OLAP, data mining) to big and unstructured data (text, social, streaming).
Emphasis is placed on the fit between data types, analytical models, and decision purposes, as well as the ability to design, evaluate, and govern analytical systems in organizations.

## Learning objectives

**Knowledge** After completing the module, students will be able to:

- **Differentiate** between descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics approaches. 🟢 [l1](slides/lecture_1_foundations_of_data_analytics.html)
- **Describe** architectures for data warehousing and big data processing, with reference to the underlying data modeling concepts. 🟢 [l2](slides/lecture_2_descriptive_analytics.html)
- **Explain** fundamental concepts of data analytics, and its role in data-driven organizations. 🟢 [l10](slides/lecture_10_data_governance.html)

**Skills** Students will be able to:

- **Select** appropriate analytical methods for transactional or non-transactional data, and for descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analysis tasks.
- **Apply** analytical methods to real-world datasets, including structuring, transforming, and visualizing data. This also involves training models, evaluating performance, and interpreting analytical results for decision-making.
- **Implement** analytical procedures in Python, using standard data science libraries (pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib).

**Competence** Upon successful completion, students will be able to:

- **Design** analytics solutions aligned with business goals and governance principles.
- **Integrate** analytical technologies into organizational decision-making processes
- **Assess** the operational and strategic implications of data analytics in organizations. 🟢 [l10](slides/lecture_10_data_governance)

## Related courses

- [Big Data & Analytics/Rossbach](https://nc-2272638881871040784.nextcloud-ionos.com/index.php/apps/files/files/1416067?dir=/teaching/materials/bda/Material/ABD)
- [Business Intelligence & Analytics (BIA), Hopf](https://nc-2272638881871040784.nextcloud-ionos.com/index.php/apps/files/files/1502189?dir=/teaching/materials/bda/Material/BIA)
- [Data-driven Decision Support (DSS), Hopf](https://nc-2272638881871040784.nextcloud-ionos.com/index.php/apps/files/files/1502191?dir=/teaching/materials/bda/Material/DSS)
- [business-analytics-fau, Hopf](https://nc-2272638881871040784.nextcloud-ionos.com/index.php/apps/files/files/1502192?dir=/teaching/materials/bda/Material/business-analytics-fau)
- [Managing Data at Scale (infs6024), Prester](https://nc-2272638881871040784.nextcloud-ionos.com/index.php/apps/files/files/1561064?dir=/teaching/materials/bda/Material/infs6024)
- [CS109A: Introduction to Data Science](https://harvard-iacs.github.io/2018-CS109A/) and [materials](https://harvard-iacs.github.io/2018-CS109A/pages/materials.html)
- [Data Science for Business](https://harvardonline.harvard.edu/course/data-science-business) with [syllabus](https://us-west-2.graphassets.com/cmcu78gdx085p08n06vc58sxv/cmi3la9wm6k7v07lk3xx2ezza)
- [UC Berkley: Data 100: Principles and Techniques of Data Science](https://ds100.org/sp26/)

## Recommended literature

- **Part 1**: @ChenEtAl2012, @MartinezEtAl2019, @ShardaEtAl2018
- **Part 2**: @Raschka2015, @SchuttONeil2014, @Vaisman2016
- **Part 3**: @BengfortEtAl2018. @Schmarzo2016
- **Part 4**: @Davenport2006, @VidgenEtAl2017


<!--
## Course Design and Pedagogical Approach

This course intentionally combines **two complementary pedagogical perspectives** to prepare students for both the *technical foundations* and the *practical realities* of Big Data and Business Analytics.

### 1. Essentialist Perspective: Core Concepts and Analytical Skills
- Focuses on **stable, foundational concepts and methods** in Big Data and Business Analytics
- Emphasizes **hard analytical skills**, models, and terminology that every graduate in the field is expected to master
- Knowledge is treated as **structured and cumulative**, building from basic principles to more advanced applications
- Learning activities include:
  - Structured lectures
  - Worked examples and problem-solving exercises
- Assessment:
  - Formal examinations and graded assignments
  - Evaluation of students’ ability to **apply core concepts correctly and rigorously**
- Learning goal:
  - Ensure a **shared analytical foundation** and methodological competence across all students

### 2. Experiential and Connectivist Perspective: Engaging with Practice
- Recognizes that Big Data and Analytics are **rapidly evolving fields** shaped by practice, tools, and professional discourse
- Treats knowledge as **distributed across networks**, including practitioners, platforms, and media
- Emphasizes **learning through experience, reflection, and sensemaking**
- Learning activities include:
  - Engagement with podcasts, practitioner articles, and business magazines
  - Short written or oral summaries connecting practice-based insights to course concepts
  - In-class discussion and collective reflection
- Assessment:
  - Low-stakes or formative evaluation focused on engagement, synthesis, and reflection
- Learning goal:
  - Develop students’ ability to **interpret real-world analytics practices**, critically relate them to theory, and stay current beyond the classroom

::: {.callout-note title="Idea"}
For the second part, one motivation could be to help everyone be better prepared for the exam.
:::

### Integrating Both Perspectives
- The essentialist component provides **conceptual clarity and analytical rigor**
- The experiential–connectivist component provides **context, relevance, and adaptability**
- Together, they support:
  - Deep understanding of Big Data concepts
  - Practical judgment in applying analytics in organizational contexts
  - Lifelong learning skills in a dynamic digital environment

TBD: also cover group-work (third part??)
-->
